pmos

Engagement — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Growth. Engagement is the middle of the lifecycle — the lever that decides whether an activated user becomes someone who lives in the product. It is the most over-instrumented and least-understood stage: every tool ships a dozen engagement charts and most measure motion, not value. The cleanest line is that engagement is the depth, frequency, and duration of interaction (distinct from adoption and retention), and it decomposes into three dimensions — frequency, depth, and breadth. The structural canon is Sarah Tavel's hierarchy of engagement (the core action → accruing benefits + mounting loss → self-perpetuating), the Hook Model (Nir Eyal: trigger → action → variable reward → investment), and engagement loops vs growth loops. Measurement runs from the blunt DAU/MAU stickiness ratio (and its "meaningful action" caveat) to the far better power user curve (Andrew Chen / Li Jin). Two debates are live: whether notification-driven engagement is health or rental, and the ethics of habit-forming design. Where the field disagrees, this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 15 verified sources.

What engagement actually is — and the lines around it

Engagement is the most slippery word in the lifecycle vocabulary because it sits between two neighbours that are easier to define. The cleanest working line comes from Userpilot's breakdown: "Engagement metrics evaluate the depth, frequency, and duration of how users interact with your product over time," whereas adoption metrics "measure whether users are reaching the features that predict long-term retention." Adoption is about reaching the value-bearing surfaces; engagement is about what happens once they're there and whether they keep coming back to do it.

The distinction that trips up most teams is engagement versus retention, and it is worth being precise. Retention asks a binary, time-windowed question — did the user come back in this period, yes or no — regardless of what they did. Engagement asks about the texture of that return: how often, how deep, across how much of the surface. A user can be retained but barely engaged (logs in monthly, does one thing), or deeply engaged in a window and then gone. The two correlate strongly but they are not the same measurement, and conflating them is how teams end up optimizing a number that moves without the business moving. Userpilot's own framing makes the dependency explicit: "Retention rate is the clearest signal that both adoption and engagement are working" — retention is the outcome; adoption and engagement are the inputs that produce it.

Braze frames engagement at the behavioural level rather than the metric level, which is the more useful starting point for a product team: product engagement is "how users interact with your product, such as how often they use it, how deeply they explore its features, and how much value they get from those experiences." The phrase "how much value they get" is doing the load-bearing work — it is the difference between engagement as a proxy for value delivered and engagement as time-on-screen. Mind the Product pushes the same point harder, defining engagement as activity "about how users interact with your product at the most granular level" while warning that the goal is "consistent, meaningful interactions that provide value to your users." Hold onto that adjective — meaningful — because the rest of this primer is, in one way or another, about not getting fooled by engagement that isn't.

The three dimensions: frequency, depth, breadth

Engagement is not a scalar, and treating it as one is the first analytical mistake. The practitioner consensus decomposes it into three roughly orthogonal dimensions, each answering a different question about how a user relates to the product.

Frequency — how often. This is the dimension most products instrument first, because it maps to the natural usage cycle: a messaging app wants daily, a tax tool wants annual, a payroll product wants every-other-Friday. Frequency is where stickiness ratios live, and where the temptation to compare yourself against the wrong benchmark is strongest — a weekly-cycle B2B tool measuring itself against a social app's daily frequency is measuring its own failure to be something it was never meant to be.

Depth (sometimes called intensity) — how much within a session. Session length, actions per session, content consumed or created. Depth is where genuine value-capture often shows up before frequency does: a user who does more each time is finding more to do, which usually precedes their deciding to come back more often.

Breadth (feature spread) — how much of the surface. The count of distinct features or surfaces a user touches in a period. Breadth is the dimension most correlated with switching cost and therefore retention, because a user who has wired three features into their workflow has three reasons to stay rather than one. Braze names the same three when it counsels tracking engagement across adoption, frequency, and depth — and crucially advises focusing on "behaviors tied to value" rather than "surface metrics like logins alone." A login is breadth-zero, depth-zero engagement; it tells you the door opened, nothing more.

The reason the decomposition matters operationally is that the three dimensions call for different interventions. Low frequency is a triggering and habit problem. Low depth is a value-density problem — the product isn't giving the user enough to do that's worth doing. Low breadth is a discovery problem — the user doesn't know the rest of the product exists, or hasn't been led to it. Mind the Product's answer to flattening these into one number is the Product Engagement Score, which deliberately combines adoption, stickiness, and growth into a composite rather than picking a single headline — a reasonable instinct, though a composite can also hide which of its inputs is moving, which is the recurring tension in engagement measurement.

The hierarchy of engagement (Sarah Tavel)

The most influential single framework for thinking about engagement as a structural property — rather than a metric — is Sarah Tavel's hierarchy of engagement, written from her vantage at Pinterest and later Benchmark. Tavel's premise is that an enduring consumer product climbs three levels, and that each level changes what the product is, not just how it's measured. As she puts it, the hierarchy has "three levels: 1) Growing engaged users, 2) Retaining users, and 3) Self-perpetuating."

The hierarchy is built bottom-up, and the foundation is the core action — the subject of the next section. Above it sits retention, which Tavel does not treat as a metric you chase but as a property you engineer through two specific forces. The top of the pyramid is the rare air: a product that has made itself self-perpetuating, where usage itself generates the conditions for more usage. The reason the model has stuck is that it reframes engagement from "get the numbers up" to "build a product that gets better and harder to leave the more it's used." That reframing is also Tavel's explicit argument against the most common engagement metric: in her telling, "Consumer social founders should ditch MAU metrics in favor of growing engaged users," because headline active-user counts measure breadth of reach, not the depth that compounds.

The core action and the engaged-user definition

Everything in Tavel's hierarchy rests on identifying one action correctly. As Mind the Product summarizes her framework, "The core action is the single action users can take that forms the foundation of your product." Pinning on Pinterest, sending a message on a messenger, completing a ride on a rideshare app — the core action is the atomic unit of value delivery, and it is the thing you should be counting when you count "engaged users." This is the discipline most teams skip: they define an engaged user as someone who opened the app, when they should define them as someone who performed the core action, ideally at the frequency that predicts retention.

The reason the core action is load-bearing is that it converts a vague aspiration ("more engagement") into a measurable, improvable target. Tavel's own framing of the payoff is direct: "when users engage with and complete the core action of the product repeatedly, it becomes more retentive for them." Repetition of the right action is what builds the second level of the hierarchy. And that second level — retention — is engineered, in Tavel's model, by two forces that any senior PM should be able to name and audit their product against. Per Mind the Product's writeup: "With accruing benefits, the more someone uses the product, the better it gets... In mounting loss, the more someone uses the product, the more they would lose if they left."

Accruing benefits and mounting loss are the two halves of a deepening relationship. Accruing benefits is the carrot — a recommendation feed that sharpens, a workspace that fills with useful history, a network that grows more valuable. Mounting loss is the switching cost — the playlists, the saved work, the social graph, the reputation that would be forfeited on leaving. A product strong on both is, in Tavel's words, on its way to "a virtuous loop that builds self-perpetuating growth." The practical audit question this yields: for your product, what accrues, and what would be lost? If the honest answer to both is "not much," your engagement is rented, not owned — which is exactly the failure mode the notifications section returns to.

The Hook Model and habit formation (Nir Eyal)

If Tavel describes the structural conditions for engagement, Nir Eyal's Hook Model describes the behavioural mechanism that produces it at the level of an individual user. In Eyal's own words, the model is "a trigger to begin using the product, an action to satisfy the trigger, a variable reward for the action, and some type of investment that, ultimately, makes the product more valuable to the user." The four phases run as a cycle, and the cycle is the point — a single pass changes nothing; repeated passes build a habit.

The two phases that distinguish the model from a generic engagement funnel are variable reward and investment. Variable reward imports the behavioural-psychology finding that unpredictable rewards are far more compulsive than predictable ones — the reason a feed that sometimes surfaces something great is stickier than one that always shows the same thing. Investment is the user putting something in — data, content, social connections, configuration — that both improves the product for them and raises the cost of leaving. Investment is, in effect, the Hook Model's version of Tavel's accruing benefits and mounting loss, operating one cycle at a time. Amplitude's gloss captures why the cycle compounds into a defensible position: hooked users "transition from requiring an external trigger to cue their product use, like an advertisement, to using it because of an internal, unconscious trigger, like an emotion."

That transition — external trigger to internal trigger — is the most strategically important idea in the model, because it is where engagement stops costing money. As Amplitude puts it, "once customers are 'hooked,' they essentially trigger themselves and are less likely to churn." Eyal describes the end state vividly: by "cycling continuously through these hooks, users begin to form associations with internal triggers," until a cemented habit is when a user subconsciously thinks "'I'm bored,' and instantly Facebook comes to mind." When the user's own emotional state is the trigger, you no longer need to buy their attention back. This is also precisely where the ethics get uncomfortable, which the model's own author has spent the years since Hooked wrestling with — covered later in this primer.

Engagement loops vs growth loops

Engagement loops and growth loops are often used interchangeably, which obscures a useful distinction. The shared parent concept is the loop itself: as ProdPad describes it, loops are "built around maintaining a continuous cycle of input, action, and output," in deliberate contrast to linear funnels that "end at the revenue stage." The loop's defining feature is that the output of one cycle becomes the input of the next, which is what makes loops compound where funnels leak.

The distinction worth holding is about what the output feeds. A growth loop reinvests output into acquisition — user-generated content gets indexed and pulls in new users, or a referral begets a new user who refers again. An engagement loop reinvests output into deeper usage by the same user — the user's action produces a result that gives them a reason to return and act again. The Hook Model is, in this sense, the canonical engagement loop: action → reward → investment → internal trigger → action. ProdPad positions the two as nested rather than opposed, treating "Engagement and retention loops" as a type of growth loop that "aim to deepen user satisfaction and reduce customer churn by keeping users actively engaged," and then notes that well-engaged users feed acquisition by being the ones who "post reviews, share their experiences."

That nesting is the practical insight: a strong engagement loop is upstream of a strong growth loop. Engaged users are the raw material that referral and content loops run on — you cannot bolt a growth loop onto a product whose users don't return often enough to generate output worth recirculating. The sequencing error to avoid is investing in viral growth mechanics before the engagement loop turns; you end up acquiring users into a product that doesn't yet hold them, which the lifecycle literature consistently describes as filling a leaky bucket.

Triggers and notifications, done right and wrong

Triggers are where the Hook Model meets its most contested tactic: the notification. The model is explicit that the goal is to migrate users from external triggers (a push, an email) to internal ones (an emotion, a routine). The danger is mistaking the scaffolding for the building — running engagement on a permanent drip of notifications and reading the resulting opens as health.

Andrew Chen's argument here is the field's sharpest counterweight, and it is worth quoting at the level he made it. When notifications carry the engagement, he writes, "it means your web product has failed to integrate itself in your user base's life, and there isn't a recurring set of traffic that you can depend on." The mechanism is a push-versus-pull distinction: "you are needing to 'push' information to the user rather than the user taking action themselves." Healthy engagement, in his framing, is the user who returns unprompted because the product earned a place in their day.

This is a genuine point of practitioner disagreement, and a senior PM should hold both sides. Chen's critique is strongest for products whose value is ambient and self-motivated — social, content, tools people would return to anyway. But there is a legitimate camp that treats notifications as functional rather than parasitic: for time-sensitive value propositions — a ride has arrived, a payment failed, a match was made, a price dropped — the notification is the value delivery, not a crutch propping up a hollow product. The honest synthesis is diagnostic, not dogmatic: the test is whether usage survives turning notifications off. If a cohort with notifications disabled retains and engages, your notifications are amplifying real demand; if that cohort collapses, your engagement was rented, and you are measuring the drip, not the product. The Hook Model's internal-trigger end state and Chen's "integrate into the user's life" are the same destination described from two directions — and notifications are a legitimate bridge to it only if the user eventually stops needing the bridge.

Measuring engagement: DAU/MAU and the stickiness ratio

The default engagement metric is the DAU/MAU ratio, and its ubiquity is matched only by how often it is misread. Mechanically it is, per Geckoboard, "the proportion of monthly active users who engage with your product in a single day window" — the share of your monthly base that shows up on an average day. As a stickiness proxy it answers "of the people who use this in a month, how many use it daily," which is a reasonable first read on whether a product has worked itself into a routine.

The benchmark numbers are worth carrying, with the caveat that they are category-dependent. Geckoboard cites Sequoia's range: the "standard DAU/MAU ratio ranges from 10-20%, with only a handful of companies exceeding 50%." Mixpanel's 2026 data puts B2B SaaS stickiness averaging around 31% across North America and EMEA — useful as a sanity check, but the more important discipline is comparing yourself against your own category's natural cycle rather than against a social app's daily rhythm. A 12% ratio is a triumph for a product used a few times a month and a disaster for a messaging app.

The two limitations are where the senior judgment lives. First, the ratio cannot see individuals: Geckoboard's own caution is that "you can't see which users are being retained and which users are churning," so a stable ratio can mask a churn-and-replace treadmill underneath. Second, and more fundamental, both terms inherit whatever weakness sits in your definition of "active." Mixpanel defines MAU as "the number of unique users who performed a meaningful action in your product within a 30-day window" — but the word "meaningful" is doing enormous work, and most implementations quietly drop it, counting an app-open as an active. Mixpanel's warning is the one to internalize: "If the users counted in that figure aren't experiencing meaningful value, the metric is closer to noise than signal." The fix is to tie "active" to the core action, not the session — which is the bridge to the better tool in the next section.

The power user curve

The single most useful upgrade over DAU/MAU is the power user curve, popularized by Andrew Chen and Li Jin. Where the ratio collapses your whole base into one number, the curve shows the distribution behind it. As a16z describes it, the power user curve is "a histogram of users' engagement by the total number of days they were active in a month, from 1 day out of the month to all 30." Chen's blunt case for it over the ratio: DAU/MAU is "a single number and so blurs this variance."

The shape is the whole point. a16z's reading: "A smile-shaped Power User Curve indicates there's a group of highly engaged users using the app daily or nearly daily." That upturn at the right edge — a cluster of people active 25-plus days a month — is the hardcore segment that signals a product has become genuinely habitual for someone. A curve that only declines, with nobody at the high-frequency end, says you have reach but no depth: lots of people try it, almost nobody lives in it. Two products with identical DAU/MAU can have opposite curves, and they are completely different businesses.

The second discipline the curve enforces is choosing the right action to plot. Chen's instruction is to "hone in on an action that maps closely to users getting specific value out of your specific product and plot the Power User Curve for that action" — not app-opens, but the core action from Tavel's framework. And the time window should match the product's natural cycle: a16z notes you use L7 for weekly-cycle products and L30 for monthly ones, so a B2B tool used on workdays isn't penalized for an empty weekend. The curve is the antidote to the stickiness ratio's blindness: it shows you not just how sticky you are on average, but for whom — and whether the engaged core is growing or hollowing out.

Engagement quality versus vanity

Every metric in this primer can be gamed, and the discipline that separates senior practitioners is refusing to optimize engagement that doesn't trace to value. Mind the Product's framing is the standard to hold to: "True engagement is about consistent, meaningful interactions that provide value to your users and, in turn, drive retention and growth." The clause "in turn, drive retention and growth" is the test — engagement that does not eventually show up as better retention or monetization is, by definition, motion without value.

The vanity trap has two common shapes. The first is counting low-value actions as engagement: logins, app-opens, page-views — Braze's "surface metrics like logins alone" — which inflate the dashboard while telling you nothing about whether value was delivered. The second, subtler trap is treating more time-on-screen as unambiguously good. For an ad-supported feed, dwell time is revenue, and the incentive is to maximize it; for a tool whose value is getting a job done, more time can mean the user is struggling, and rising session length is a bug report, not a win. The same number is a triumph in one business model and a failure in another, which is why engagement metrics cannot be lifted from someone else's playbook.

The constructive move is to tie every engagement metric back to a value-bearing behaviour and to prefer composite or distributional views over single headline numbers. Mixpanel's "closer to noise than signal" warning and Braze's "behaviors tied to value" counsel are the same instruction from two vendors: instrument the core action, not the session; watch the power user curve's shape, not just the stickiness ratio's level; and be suspicious of any engagement gain that doesn't propagate to retention within a cohort or two. An engagement win that never reaches retention was never a win — it was a measurement artifact.

The ethics of engagement

Engagement is the one lifecycle stage where the most effective tactics and the most ethically fraught ones substantially overlap. The variable reward that makes a feed compulsive is the same mechanism that makes it hard to put down; the internal trigger that ends re-acquisition cost is the same one that can colonize a user's idle moments. This is contested territory, and notably the loudest critic of habit-forming design is the person who codified it — Nir Eyal has spent the years since Hooked arguing that the same techniques demand an ethical test, and even wrote a follow-up, Indistractable, about resisting them.

Eyal's own tool for the question is the Manipulation Matrix, which turns on two questions: "Will I use the product myself?" and "Will the product help users materially improve their lives?" The two answers place a maker into one of four quadrants — facilitator (uses it, improves lives), entertainer (uses it, doesn't claim to improve lives), peddler (doesn't use it, claims to improve lives), and dealer (neither). His ethical bar is stated plainly: "You have to want to use the product and believe it materially benefits your life as well as the lives of your users." The facilitator is the only unambiguously defensible quadrant — and Eyal's pointed inversion of the drug-dealer maxim is that ethical makers should get high on their own supply.

A senior PM should treat this as a live design constraint rather than a compliance footnote, and also know that critics find the matrix too self-serving to be a real safeguard — a team that believes its product improves lives can rationalize almost anything, and "would I use it" is a low bar for people already selected for loving the product. The more durable guardrail is the one this whole primer has been circling: engagement that reflects genuine value delivered is both the more ethical and the more defensible kind, because it survives the user becoming more deliberate about their attention. Engagement extracted against the user's interests is not only ethically exposed; it is commercially fragile, the first thing a user cuts when they take stock — which is the same fragility Chen identified in notification-rented retention, arriving from the ethical direction instead of the metrics one.

Choosing your engagement metric

The synthesis question every PM eventually faces is: of all these, which number do we actually steer by? There is no universal answer, but there is a defensible procedure, and it assembles the pieces of this primer in order.

Start by naming the core action, in Tavel's sense — "the single action users can take that forms the foundation of your product." Define an "engaged user" as someone who performs it at the frequency that predicts retention for your category, not as someone who merely opened the app. This is the step that immunizes you against Mixpanel's "noise than signal" failure, because it forces "active" to mean something value-bearing.

Then choose the view that matches your usage cycle and resist the single headline number. The DAU/MAU ratio is a fine vital sign but a poor steering metric because, as Geckoboard notes, it hides who is churning; the power user curve is the better steering instrument because its shape tells you whether your engaged core is thickening or thinning. Use frequency, depth, and breadth as a diagnostic triad when the headline moves — they tell you which kind of engagement changed and therefore what to do. And weigh whatever you choose against the quality test: Tavel's own conclusion, after all the metrics, is to grow engaged users completing the core action repeatedly, because "when users engage with and complete the core action of the product repeatedly, it becomes more retentive for them." The metric you steer by should be the one whose improvement you would still believe in if every vanity dashboard went dark — the one that, when it moves, retention reliably follows.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Retention — engagement is the input; retention is the output. The retention curve, cohort analysis, and the DAU/MAU "active" trap are one hop down from here.
  • Activation & aha moments — engagement assumes the user has already reached first value; activation is the upstream stage that gets them there, and a weak activation rate caps the engagement ceiling.
  • Growth loops & network effects — engaged users are the fuel that referral and content loops run on; the self-perpetuating top of Tavel's hierarchy is where engagement and growth merge.
  • North-star metrics — the engaged-user definition and the core action are typically the raw material a north-star metric is built from.
  • Behavioural economics in product — variable rewards, internal triggers, and the Hook Model sit on a deeper literature of habit formation worth its own study.
  • Resurrection & win-back — the lifecycle stage for users whose engagement has already lapsed, and the counter-camp on whether chasing them is worth it.

References

  1. sarahtavel.medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Sarah Tavel's hierarchy of engagement has "three levels: 1) Growing engaged users, 2) Retaining users, and 3) Self-perpetuating"; as a product climbs, it becomes better, harder to leave, and eventually self-perpetuating through loops — her core argument being that consumer-product builders should obsess over growing *engaged* users completing the core action rather than headline MAU.

  2. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Tavel's mechanics: "The core action is the single action users can take that forms the foundation of your product"; retention comes from two forces — "With accruing benefits, the more someone uses the product, the better it gets... In mounting loss, the more someone uses the product, the more they would lose if they left" — and an enduring product takes users up all three levels, ending in "a virtuous loop that builds self-perpetuating growth."

  3. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    In Tavel's framing, "Consumer social founders should ditch MAU metrics in favor of growing engaged users" because "when users engage with and complete the core action of the product repeatedly, it becomes more retentive for them" — engagement, not headcount, is the fuel for an enduring product, and the tipping point shows up as rising cohort retention plus organic word of mouth.

  4. amplitude.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Nir Eyal's Hook Model has users "repeatedly pass through the four stages of the 'hook': trigger, action, variable reward, and investment"; the strategic shift is that hooked users "transition from requiring an external trigger to cue their product use, like an advertisement, to using it because of an internal, unconscious trigger, like an emotion," so "once customers are 'hooked,' they essentially trigger themselves and are less likely to churn" — habit substitutes for paid re-acquisition.

  5. nirandfar.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Eyal's own definition: the Hooked Model is "a trigger to begin using the product, an action to satisfy the trigger, a variable reward for the action, and some type of investment that, ultimately, makes the product more valuable to the user"; "By cycling continuously through these hooks, users begin to form associations with internal triggers," so a cemented habit is when a user subconsciously thinks "'I'm bored,' and instantly Facebook comes to mind."

  6. a16z.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    The power user curve (Chen / Li Jin) is "a histogram of users' engagement by the total number of days they were active in a month, from 1 day out of the month to all 30"; "A smile-shaped Power User Curve indicates there's a group of highly engaged users using the app daily or nearly daily," and it reveals heterogeneity a single DAU/MAU number hides — use L7 for weekly-cycle products and L30 for monthly.

  7. andrewchen.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Chen argues DAU/MAU is "a single number and so blurs this variance"; the power user curve instead "shows the characteristic smile shape that indicates there's a group of highly engaged users using the app daily or nearly daily," and you should "hone in on an action that maps closely to users getting specific value out of your specific product and plot the Power User Curve for that action" rather than for raw opens.

  8. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Product engagement "is about how users interact with your product at the most granular level," and "True engagement is about consistent, meaningful interactions that provide value to your users and, in turn, drive retention and growth" — the article pushes builders past raw frequency counts toward a Product Engagement Score combining adoption, stickiness, and growth, prioritizing quality of interaction over quantity of usage.

  9. andrewchen.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Chen's caution on notification-driven engagement: when pushes carry the engagement, "it means your web product has failed to integrate itself in your user base's life, and there isn't a recurring set of traffic that you can depend on" — "you are needing to 'push' information to the user rather than the user taking action themselves" — the healthy version is users who "come in day-in-and-day out" on their own.

  10. userpilot.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    The distinction: "Engagement metrics evaluate the depth, frequency, and duration of how users interact with your product over time," whereas "Adoption metrics measure whether users are reaching the features that predict long-term retention," and "Retention rate is the clearest signal that both adoption and engagement are working" — a user can be highly engaged yet low-adoption (or vice versa), and each combination signals a different problem.

  11. geckoboard.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    DAU/MAU is "the proportion of monthly active users who engage with your product in a single day window" and is the standard stickiness measure; per Sequoia the "standard DAU/MAU ratio ranges from 10-20%, with only a handful of companies exceeding 50%." Its key blind spot: "you can't see which users are being retained and which users are churning," so it needs cohort retention analysis alongside it.

  12. mixpanel.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Mixpanel defines MAU as "the number of unique users who performed a meaningful action in your product within a 30-day window" and DAU/MAU as "the most widely used measure of product stickiness"; its warning is blunt — "If the users counted in that figure aren't experiencing meaningful value, the metric is closer to noise than signal" — with 2026 B2B SaaS stickiness averaging ~31% across North America and EMEA.

  13. prodpad.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Growth loops are "built around maintaining a continuous cycle of input, action, and output" — unlike linear AAARRR funnels that "end at the revenue stage" — and "Engagement and retention loops" are a *type* of growth loop that "aim to deepen user satisfaction and reduce customer churn by keeping users actively engaged," then feed well-engaged users into acquisition via referrals and reviews. Engagement is the loop's middle; output is what makes it compound.

  14. braze.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Braze defines product engagement as "how users interact with your product, such as how often they use it, how deeply they explore its features, and how much value they get" — tracked across adoption, frequency, and depth — and counsels focusing on "behaviors tied to value" rather than "surface metrics like logins alone," guiding users "based on what they've already done" instead of showing every feature up front.

  15. nirandfar.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Eyal's Manipulation Matrix asks two questions — "Will I use the product myself?" and "Will the product help users materially improve their lives?" — placing makers as facilitator, entertainer, peddler, or dealer; his ethical bar is that "You have to want to use the product and believe it materially benefits your life as well as the lives of your users," because "Attempting to create a persuasive technology that you don't find valuable enough to use yourself is nearly impossible."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_engagement.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
